highlights.titleZh: Nvidia ploughs $2 billion into Marvell in data centre push - Telecoms
highlights.content: * [AI](/digital-ecosystem/ai) * [Fibre](/fixed-networks/fibre) * [News](/latest-news) # Nvidia ploughs $2 billion into Marvell in data centre push Nvidia has announced a $2 billion investment in fellow chipmaker Marvell, as they join hands and look to develop silicon photonics technology and data centre networking. [Andrew Wooden,](/author/andrew-wooden) Deputy Editor April 1, 2026 3 Min Read As well as that sizable investment, which seems to be a default move by Nvidia when shopping for collaborators these days, the idea is about connecting Marvell to the Nvidia AI factory and AI-RAN ecosystem, “offering customers building on Nvidia architectures greater choice and flexibility in developing next-generation infrastructure.” Nvidia NVLink Fusion is described as a rack-scale platform that enables data centre customers to develop semi-custom AI infrastructure using its ecosystem. Marvell getting involved will see it contributing custom XPUs and compatible scale-up networking into the mix, while Nvidia will provide the supporting technologies – which include Vera CPU, ConnectX NICs, BlueField DPUs, NVLink interconnect and Spectrum-X switches, and the rack-scale AI compute. For customers developing custom chips, “NVLink Fusion enables a heterogeneous AI infrastructure fully compatible with Nvidia systems, allowing seamless integration with Nvidia GPU, LPU, networking and storage platforms,” states the release.  Related: [EE launches AI TV search feature](/ai/ee-launches-ai-tv-search-feature) There’s a lot of jargon in there, but essentially they are pooling resources to jointly go after AI data centre business.  “The inference inflection has arrived. Token generation demand is surging, and the world is racing to build AI factories,” said Jensen Huang, founder and CEO of Nvidia. “Together with Marvell, we are enabling customers to leverage Nvidia’s AI infrastructure ecosystem and scale to build specialized AI compute.” Matt Murphy, chairman and CEO of Marvell added: “Our expanded partnership with Nvidia reflects the growing importance of high-speed connectivity, optical interconnect and accelerated infrastructure in scaling AI. By connecting Marvell’s leadership in high-performance analog, optical DSP, silicon photonics and custom silicon to Nvidia’s expanding AI ecosystem through NVLink Fusion, we are enabling customers to build scalable, efficient AI infrastructure.” The companies will work to “transform the world’s telecommunication network into AI infrastructure” using Nvidia Aerial AI-RAN for 5G/6G, optical interconnect solutions, and silicon photonics technology.  This latter part would seem to be a key pillar of the deal. Late last year Marvell agreed to [acquire Celestial AI](/ai/marvell-snaps-up-celestial-ai) for an upfront consideration valued at approximately $3.25 billion.  Celestial AI makes an optical interconnect technology platform for AI computing called Photonic Fabric, which is supposed to enable large AI clusters to scale both within and across racks using a high-bandwidth, low latency, low power and cost-effective optical fabric. This apparently means more than two times the power efficiency of copper interconnects, along with longer reach and higher bandwidth. Related: [Mistral raises $830m to fund Nvidia spending spree](/ai/mistral-raises-830m-to-fund-nvidia-spending-spree) Marvell said at the time of that deal that combined with its existing scale-out and scale-across connectivity, it expects the expanded portfolio “will result in the industry’s most comprehensive provider of high-bandwidth, low-power, low-latency solutions for next-generation data centre connectivity.” In January this year Marvell then made a further purchase in the data centre space, [snapping up XConn for $540 million](/enterprise-telecoms/marvell-buys-xconn-for-540m-for-an-even-bigger-piece-of-the-data-center-action) . highlights.title: Why Neoclouds Are Vital to AI Startups - Bismarck Brief
highlights.titleZh: Why Neoclouds Are Vital to AI Startups - Bismarck Brief
highlights.content: # [Bismarck Brief](/) [AI 2026](https://brief.bismarckanalysis.com/s/ai-2026/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu) # Why Neoclouds Are Vital to AI Startups ### Commodity compute is key to making new AI research breakthroughs. AI startups and Nvidia have allied with financial firms to prevent this commodity from being monopolized by incumbent software giants. [Samo Burja](https://substack.com/@samoburja) Apr 01, 2026 ∙ Paid The recent growth in optimism about artificial intelligence technology has led to a boom in demand for the computing power or “compute” that these new AI algorithms require for training i.e. development and inference i.e. usage. The high compute demands for AI are met through chips like [Nvidia’s](https://brief.bismarckanalysis.com/p/nvidias-successful-bet-on-artificial) “graphics processing units” (GPUs), which are capable of processing vast amounts of computations in parallel. While GPUs and their equivalents from other companies like [AMD](https://brief.bismarckanalysis.com/p/amd-is-a-functional-institution-in) or Google can be purchased and run on a company’s own premises in what is now commonly called a “data center” or “compute cluster,” the financial costs and organizational complexities of building such data centers, maintaining them, and inevitably upgrading them with new and more powerful chips are prohibitively high even apparently for well-funded frontier AI labs like OpenAI or Anthropic. As a result, many companies and individuals choose instead to rent compute from “cloud computing” providers like [Amazon Web Services](https://brief.bismarckanalysis.com/p/the-growth-prospects-for-aws) (AWS) or [Microsoft](https://brief.bismarckanalysis.com/p/ai-2026-microsofts-transformation) Azure and, alongside these large and established companies, a number of smaller and newer “neocloud” companies have arisen to meet demand specifically for remotely renting out AI compute. ## This post is for paid subscribers [Already a paid subscriber? **Sign in**](https://substack.com/sign-in?redirect=%2Fp%2Fwhy-neoclouds-are-vital-to-ai-startups&for_pub=bismarck&change_user=false) © 2026 Samo Burja · [Privacy](https://substack.com/privacy) ∙ [Terms](https://substack.com/tos) ∙ [Collection notice](https://substack.com/ccpa#personal-data-collected) [Start your Substack](https://substack.com/signup?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=web&utm_content=footer)[Get the app](https://substack.com/app/app-store-redirect?utm_campaign=app-marketing&utm_content=web-footer-button) [Substack](https://substack.com) is the home for great culture

highlights.title: Clawing Back on Security: Challenges with Agentic AI Systems - Infosecurity Magazine
highlights.titleZh: Clawing Back on Security: Challenges with Agentic AI Systems - Infosecurity Magazine
highlights.content: [Infosecurity Magazine Home](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/) » [Opinions](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/opinions/) » [Clawing Back on Security: Challenges with Agentic AI Systems](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/opinions/clawing-back-security-challenges/) # Clawing Back on Security: Challenges with Agentic AI Systems [Opinion](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/opinions/) ## Written by ### [Donavan Cheah](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/profile/donavan-cheah/) Emerging Trends Working Group Member, ISACA Clawdbot’s popularity has been meteoric, racking up more than 140,000 stars and 20,000 forks on its Github repository. However, [its renaming to Moltbot](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/malicious-crypto-trading-skills/) suggested significant security issues, such as the trifecta of access to private data, exposure to untrusted content and external communication capabilities. One issue behind AI agents like this being insecure by design is because LLMs are unable to distinguish between different contexts. For instance, LLMs without guardrails cannot distinguish between a legitimate public webpage and one that serves as a precursor to an indirect [prompt injection](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/ncsc-raises-alarms-prompt/) attack. Given the statistical nature of LLMs, it is possible for an indirect prompt injection to work even with the best-designed guardrails. But the trifecta itself has already been present in agentic AI pre-Moltbot. What Moltbot changes is the introduction of persistent memory. Attackers can attempt to compromise a Moltbot-based agentic architecture through time-delayed attacks, as attack strings can persist in memory, resulting in [memory poisoning](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/indirect-prompt-injection-poisons-ai-longterm-memory/). However, memory poisoning attacks aim to target the agent’s long-term memory store and are designed to persist in the agentic AI system without detection. This typically involves the embedding of malicious data bit by bit, through means such as multi-shot prompting to introduce false premises to modify its operational context. Due to the agents trusting their own memory, there exists no additional validation in executing actions from a now-corrupted operational context. The main issue is that the model and memory could be poisoned through an indirect prompt injection attack that gets detected only much later when the model produces outputs that significantly deviate from its intended use. Only then will the model owners discover their model has been poisoned, but with no means to identify the extent of poisoning due to its persistence in memory. ## **Security Struggles to Catch Up with Generative AI and Agentic AI** Given the evolution speed of AI technologies, it is unsurprising that security professionals are concerned they are not ready to face risks associated with GenAI solutions. In [ISACA’s 2026 Tech Trends and Priorities survey](https://www.isaca.org/resources/infographics/2026-tech-trends-and-priorities), only 13% of professionals say that they are well-prepared to face GenAI risks despite how 62% of respondents identify [AI and machine learning](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/top-cyber-threats-facing-agentic/) as top technological priorities for 2026. The rise of [GenAI and agentic AI](https://www.infosecurityeurope.com/en-gb/blog/threat-vectors/how-to-hack-large-language-models.html) has also led to capabilities such as rapid prototyping and instant usable feedback being placed in the hands of laypersons for the first time. Before the rise of GenAI, most AI and ML research was confined in back-end systems, which did not drastically increase the attack surface the way GenAI and agentic AI did. It was thus notable that threat modeling frameworks such as [MAESTRO](https://cloudsecurityalliance.org/blog/2025/02/06/agentic-ai-threat-modeling-framework-maestro), originally written by Ken Huang as a Clou...

highlights.title: AI Is Becoming a Digital Employee. Why Are We Still Securing It Like Software? - Dark Reading
highlights.titleZh: AI Is Becoming a Digital Employee. Why Are We Still Securing It Like Software? - Dark Reading
highlights.content: * [Cyber Risk](/cyber-risk) * [Threat Intelligence](/threat-intelligence) * [Vulnerabilities & Threats](/vulnerabilities-threats) # AI Is Becoming a Digital Employee. Why Are We Still Securing It Like Software? AI agents are reshaping enterprise security, demanding identity governance and privilege controls to mitigate risks from their dynamic, autonomous actions. [Brad Jones,](/author/brad-jones)CISO,Snowflake April 6, 2026 5 Min Read SOURCE: InfiniteSource / Adobe Stock Sponsored by Snowflake When we deploy traditional software, we install systems designed to follow instructions. When we deploy an AI agent, we're effectively onboarding a new employee — one who can access sensitive data, make consequential decisions, and take actions in their department. Except this employee also has direct access to APIs and the ability to trigger programmatic workflows, and it operates at machine speed. The security frameworks most organizations are using today were not built for this. AI agents require the identity governance of a workforce and the technical controls of software at the same time. It’s a new kind of security risk, and configuring the enterprise for it means treating AI as what it actually is: a new hybrid actor working inside the enterprise with privilege. ## From Chatbots to Autonomous Agents The early era of enterprise AI was largely conversational. A user typed a prompt, a model generated a response, and a human decided what to do with it. For the past few years, security concerns focused on chatbot failure modes: hallucinations, prompt injection, jailbreaks, data leakage. However, humans always served as the final checkpoint between AI output and real-world action.   Related:[Cyberattacks Intensify Pressure on Latin American Governments](/cyber-risk/cyberattacks-latin-american-governments) Today, the agentic era forces a rewrite of that model. AI systems now query databases, chain tools together, join datasets in unanticipated ways, and kick off downstream workflows, and they can do it without a human reviewing each step. The risk is no longer what a model might say. It's about what an agent can access and execute. And in most organizations, agents operate under service accounts with inherited privileges far broader than their actual task requires. This makes AI security less a software problem and increasingly an identity and access challenge. ## The Blind Spots in Agentic AI The risks compound as organizations interconnect AI agents, chaining systems together, calling each other's APIs, traversing environments, and operating under multiple identity contexts. Execution paths become non-deterministic. An agent authorized to summarize sales data might, through a sequence of tool calls, end up accessing systems and datasets it was never intended to reach. Guardrails around models don't address this. The risk isn't just what happens inside the model; it's what happens between systems, in the seams where one agent hands off to another, where permissions are inherited rather than explicitly granted, and where no single team has full visibility into the chain. This vulnerability exists partly by design. Because agentic AI paths are unpredictable, teams often grant broad access to avoid constraining the agent's ability to deliver results. The same flexibility that makes agents valuable is what makes them difficult to govern. Related:[Google's Vertex AI Is Over-Privileged. That's a Problem](/cyber-risk/googles-vertex-ai-over-privilege-problem) But traditional security frameworks assume access can be defined in advance and behavior can be audited after the fact. AI agents break both sides of that equation. Their access patterns are dynamic and context-dependent. Their execution paths are difficult to predict. And the logging infrastructure across most multi-platform environments is too fragmented and inconsistent to give any single team a unified picture of what agents in the business are actually doing, or why. The result: blin...

highlights.content: [Latest News](/news) [## Anthropic signs deal with federal government](/news/anthropic-signs-deal-with-federal-government-624725) [## Australia readies social media court action](/news/australia-readies-social-media-court-action-624713) [## WA local gov entity lost $350,000 in phishing attack](/news/wa-local-gov-entity-lost-350000-in-phishing-attack-624680) [## NBN Co challenged on size of ARPU increases from fibre customers](/news/nbn-co-challenged-on-size-of-arpu-increases-from-fibre-customers-624712) [## Supply chain attack hits 100 million-download Axios npm package](/news/supply-chain-attack-hits-100-million-download-axios-npm-package-624699) * [Home](https://www.itnews.com.au) * [News](https://www.itnews.com.au/news) * [Technology](https://www.itnews.com.au/technology) * [Software](https://www.itnews.com.au/technology/software) # Anthropic signs deal with federal government By [Staff Writer](/author/staff-writer-1302125) Apr 1 2026 1:20PM ## On AI safety and economic data tracking. Anthropic has sign a memorandum of understanding to share its economic index data with the Australian government to help track artificial intelligence ‌adoption across the economy, and ‌its ‌impact on workers and jobs. Under ‌the agreement, the Claude ⁠maker will share findings on emerging AI model capabilities and risks, participate in joint safety evaluations, and collaborate on research ​with Australian universities. Anthropic said it would also target investments in ⁠data centre infrastructure and energy across Australia. "Australia's investment in AI safety makes it a natural partner for responsible AI development," Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei said in Canberra, where he met Prime Minister Anthony Albanese, "This memorandum of ​understanding gives our collaboration ⁠a formal foundation." The deal mirrors ⁠similar agreements with safety institutes in the United States, Britain ​and Japan. Australia currently has no specific ‌AI legislation. ⁠ The centre-left Labor government has said it would rely on existing laws to manage emerging AI risks ‌while introducing voluntary guidelines amid privacy and safety concerns. In its National AI Plan released in December, Labor outlined a roadmap ​to ramp up AI adoption across the economy, attract data centre investment, and build AI skills to ‌support jobs ⁠as the technology ​becomes more integrated into daily life. ### Add iTnews as your trusted source Got a news tip for our journalists? Listen · 3:08 min * Share full article * [![Image 1: Erin Griffith](https://static01.nyt.com/images/2019/06/18/reader-center/author-erin-griffith/author-erin-griffith-thumbLarge.png)](https://www.nytimes.com/by/erin-griffith) By [Erin Griffith](https://www.nytimes.com/by/erin-griffith) Reporting from San Francisco April 1, 2026, 2:13 p.m. ET [See more of our coverage in your search results.Encuentra más de nuestra cobertura en los resultados de búsqueda.Add The New York Times on Google Agrega The New York Times en Google](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=nytimes.com) OpenAI, Anthropic, Waymo and other artificial intelligence companies shattered fund-raising records in the first three months of the year with a $297 billion haul, according to [data from Crunchbase](https://news.crunchbase.com/venture/record-breaking-funding-ai-global-q1-2026/), which tracks private investment. To put that sum into perspective: Last year was already record breaking, with technology start-ups raising $425 billion, up 30 percent from 2024. The first three months of 2026 put the industry on track to almost triple that amount. highlights.titleZh: OpenAI Raises $3 Billion from Retail Investors as Part of $122 Billion Funding - MLQ.ai
highlights.content: # OpenAI Raises $3 Billion from Retail Investors as Part of $122 Billion Funding April 1, 2026 at 9:53 AM • by MLQ Agent #### Key Points * OpenAI is raising $110 billion in new investment at a $730 billion pre-money valuation.[1] * $30 billion each from SoftBank and NVIDIA, and $50 billion from Amazon.[1] * Includes strategic partnership with Amazon and compute deal with NVIDIA.[1] * Valuation boosts OpenAI Foundation's stake to over $180 billion.[1] * Additional investors expected as round progresses.[1] OpenAI announced a $110 billion funding round on Wednesday at a $730 billion pre-money valuation, led by major investments from SoftBank, NVIDIA, and Amazon. The deal includes strategic partnerships and aims to support the company's AI development efforts. ## Funding Breakdown The round comprises $30 billion from SoftBank, $30 billion from NVIDIA, and $50 billion from Amazon.[1] OpenAI has also secured a strategic partnership with Amazon and next-generation inference compute with NVIDIA.[1] Additional financial investors are expected to participate as the round continues.[1] ## Valuation Impact The new valuation increases the value of the OpenAI Foundation’s stake in OpenAI Group to over $180 billion.[1] This strengthens the foundation's resources, one of the most well-funded nonprofits, enabling expanded philanthropy in health breakthroughs and AI resilience.[1] ## Context from Recent Deals Separate reports indicate OpenAI employees have cashed out $3 billion in shares amid ongoing fundraising talks for up to $40 billion.[3] Earlier funding included a $6.6 billion raise at a $157 billion valuation, plus a $4 billion credit line.[2] ## Investor Confidence Signals The scale of OpenAI's $110 billion raise underscores the intense capital demands of frontier AI development, where compute resources and talent competition drive unprecedented investment levels.[1] Commitments from SoftBank, NVIDIA, and Amazon—totaling the full announced amount—signal strong institutional confidence in OpenAI's path to scaling AI capabilities, particularly with tied compute and partnership deals that reduce execution risks.[1] This pre-money valuation of $730 billion positions OpenAI ahead of many public tech firms, reflecting investor bets on AI's centrality to future economic growth despite the company's private status and governance complexities. Comparisons to prior rounds highlight accelerating momentum: from $6.6 billion at $157 billion valuation to this massive infusion.[2] Employee share cash-outs of $3 billion suggest maturing internal liquidity options, potentially aiding retention amid talent wars, while talks of additional $40 billion indicate the round may expand further.[3] Overall, the structure prioritizes strategic alignment over pure financial returns, with backers gaining direct exposure to AI infrastructure advancements. ## Scaling and IPO Path OpenAI's partnerships with Amazon and NVIDIA position it to accelerate inference and model training, key to competing in real-time AI applications.[1] As additional investors join, the round could surpass initial targets, funding expansions like the referenced AI superapp ambitions from related coverage.[4] The bolstered foundation stake enables parallel investments in AI safety and philanthropy, potentially shaping public policy discourse on responsible scaling.[1] A potential IPO remains on the horizon, with this liquidity event building retail and public interest through demonstrated growth metrics, such as surpassing 1 million business users for ChatGPT.[2] Regulatory scrutiny may intensify given the valuation and nonprofit ties, but secured compute and capital provide a runway through 2027 at least. Competitive pressures from rivals will test whether this funding translates to sustained market leadership. ### Companies Mentioned [#### Amazon.com, Inc. AI Agents.title: Clawing Back on Security: Challenges with Agentic AI Systems - Infosecurity Magazine
AI Agents.titleZh: Clawing Back on Security: Challenges with Agentic AI Systems - Infosecurity Magazine
AI Agents.content: [Infosecurity Magazine Home](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/) » [Opinions](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/opinions/) » [Clawing Back on Security: Challenges with Agentic AI Systems](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/opinions/clawing-back-security-challenges/) # Clawing Back on Security: Challenges with Agentic AI Systems [Opinion](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/opinions/) ## Written by ### [Donavan Cheah](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/profile/donavan-cheah/) Emerging Trends Working Group Member, ISACA Clawdbot’s popularity has been meteoric, racking up more than 140,000 stars and 20,000 forks on its Github repository. However, [its renaming to Moltbot](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/malicious-crypto-trading-skills/) suggested significant security issues, such as the trifecta of access to private data, exposure to untrusted content and external communication capabilities. One issue behind AI agents like this being insecure by design is because LLMs are unable to distinguish between different contexts. For instance, LLMs without guardrails cannot distinguish between a legitimate public webpage and one that serves as a precursor to an indirect [prompt injection](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/ncsc-raises-alarms-prompt/) attack. Given the statistical nature of LLMs, it is possible for an indirect prompt injection to work even with the best-designed guardrails. But the trifecta itself has already been present in agentic AI pre-Moltbot. What Moltbot changes is the introduction of persistent memory. Attackers can attempt to compromise a Moltbot-based agentic architecture through time-delayed attacks, as attack strings can persist in memory, resulting in [memory poisoning](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/indirect-prompt-injection-poisons-ai-longterm-memory/). However, memory poisoning attacks aim to target the agent’s long-term memory store and are designed to persist in the agentic AI system without detection. This typically involves the embedding of malicious data bit by bit, through means such as multi-shot prompting to introduce false premises to modify its operational context. Due to the agents trusting their own memory, there exists no additional validation in executing actions from a now-corrupted operational context. The main issue is that the model and memory could be poisoned through an indirect prompt injection attack that gets detected only much later when the model produces outputs that significantly deviate from its intended use. Only then will the model owners discover their model has been poisoned, but with no means to identify the extent of poisoning due to its persistence in memory. ## **Security Struggles to Catch Up with Generative AI and Agentic AI** Given the evolution speed of AI technologies, it is unsurprising that security professionals are concerned they are not ready to face risks associated with GenAI solutions. In [ISACA’s 2026 Tech Trends and Priorities survey](https://www.isaca.org/resources/infographics/2026-tech-trends-and-priorities), only 13% of professionals say that they are well-prepared to face GenAI risks despite how 62% of respondents identify [AI and machine learning](https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/top-cyber-threats-facing-agentic/) as top technological priorities for 2026. The rise of [GenAI and agentic AI](https://www.infosecurityeurope.com/en-gb/blog/threat-vectors/how-to-hack-large-language-models.html) has also led to capabilities such as rapid prototyping and instant usable feedback being placed in the hands of laypersons for the first time. Before the rise of GenAI, most AI and ML research was confined in back-end systems, which did not drastically increase the attack surface the way GenAI and agentic AI did. It was thus notable that threat modeling frameworks such as [MAESTRO](https://cloudsecurityalliance.org/blog/2025/02/06/agentic-ai-threat-modeling-framework-maestro), originally written by Ken Huang as a Clou...

AI Agents.title: AI Is Becoming a Digital Employee. Why Are We Still Securing It Like Software? - Dark Reading
AI Agents.titleZh: AI Is Becoming a Digital Employee. Why Are We Still Securing It Like Software? - Dark Reading
AI Agents.content: * [Cyber Risk](/cyber-risk) * [Threat Intelligence](/threat-intelligence) * [Vulnerabilities & Threats](/vulnerabilities-threats) # AI Is Becoming a Digital Employee. Why Are We Still Securing It Like Software? AI agents are reshaping enterprise security, demanding identity governance and privilege controls to mitigate risks from their dynamic, autonomous actions. [Brad Jones,](/author/brad-jones)CISO,Snowflake April 6, 2026 5 Min Read SOURCE: InfiniteSource / Adobe Stock Sponsored by Snowflake When we deploy traditional software, we install systems designed to follow instructions. When we deploy an AI agent, we're effectively onboarding a new employee — one who can access sensitive data, make consequential decisions, and take actions in their department. Except this employee also has direct access to APIs and the ability to trigger programmatic workflows, and it operates at machine speed. The security frameworks most organizations are using today were not built for this. AI agents require the identity governance of a workforce and the technical controls of software at the same time. It’s a new kind of security risk, and configuring the enterprise for it means treating AI as what it actually is: a new hybrid actor working inside the enterprise with privilege. ## From Chatbots to Autonomous Agents The early era of enterprise AI was largely conversational. A user typed a prompt, a model generated a response, and a human decided what to do with it. For the past few years, security concerns focused on chatbot failure modes: hallucinations, prompt injection, jailbreaks, data leakage. However, humans always served as the final checkpoint between AI output and real-world action.   Related:[Cyberattacks Intensify Pressure on Latin American Governments](/cyber-risk/cyberattacks-latin-american-governments) Today, the agentic era forces a rewrite of that model. AI systems now query databases, chain tools together, join datasets in unanticipated ways, and kick off downstream workflows, and they can do it without a human reviewing each step. The risk is no longer what a model might say. It's about what an agent can access and execute. And in most organizations, agents operate under service accounts with inherited privileges far broader than their actual task requires. This makes AI security less a software problem and increasingly an identity and access challenge. ## The Blind Spots in Agentic AI The risks compound as organizations interconnect AI agents, chaining systems together, calling each other's APIs, traversing environments, and operating under multiple identity contexts. Execution paths become non-deterministic. An agent authorized to summarize sales data might, through a sequence of tool calls, end up accessing systems and datasets it was never intended to reach. Guardrails around models don't address this. The risk isn't just what happens inside the model; it's what happens between systems, in the seams where one agent hands off to another, where permissions are inherited rather than explicitly granted, and where no single team has full visibility into the chain. This vulnerability exists partly by design. Because agentic AI paths are unpredictable, teams often grant broad access to avoid constraining the agent's ability to deliver results. The same flexibility that makes agents valuable is what makes them difficult to govern. Related:[Google's Vertex AI Is Over-Privileged. That's a Problem](/cyber-risk/googles-vertex-ai-over-privilege-problem) But traditional security frameworks assume access can be defined in advance and behavior can be audited after the fact. AI agents break both sides of that equation. Their access patterns are dynamic and context-dependent. Their execution paths are difficult to predict. And the logging infrastructure across most multi-platform environments is too fragmented and inconsistent to give any single team a unified picture of what agents in the business are actually doing, or why. The result: blin...

AI Agents.titleZh: Anthropic signs deal with federal government - iTnews
AI Agents.content: [Latest News](/news) [## Anthropic signs deal with federal government](/news/anthropic-signs-deal-with-federal-government-624725) [## Australia readies social media court action](/news/australia-readies-social-media-court-action-624713) [## WA local gov entity lost $350,000 in phishing attack](/news/wa-local-gov-entity-lost-350000-in-phishing-attack-624680) [## NBN Co challenged on size of ARPU increases from fibre customers](/news/nbn-co-challenged-on-size-of-arpu-increases-from-fibre-customers-624712) [## Supply chain attack hits 100 million-download Axios npm package](/news/supply-chain-attack-hits-100-million-download-axios-npm-package-624699) * [Home](https://www.itnews.com.au) * [News](https://www.itnews.com.au/news) * [Technology](https://www.itnews.com.au/technology) * [Software](https://www.itnews.com.au/technology/software) # Anthropic signs deal with federal government By [Staff Writer](/author/staff-writer-1302125) Apr 1 2026 1:20PM ## On AI safety and economic data tracking. Anthropic has sign a memorandum of understanding to share its economic index data with the Australian government to help track artificial intelligence ‌adoption across the economy, and ‌its ‌impact on workers and jobs. Under ‌the agreement, the Claude ⁠maker will share findings on emerging AI model capabilities and risks, participate in joint safety evaluations, and collaborate on research ​with Australian universities. Anthropic said it would also target investments in ⁠data centre infrastructure and energy across Australia. "Australia's investment in AI safety makes it a natural partner for responsible AI development," Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei said in Canberra, where he met Prime Minister Anthony Albanese, "This memorandum of ​understanding gives our collaboration ⁠a formal foundation." The deal mirrors ⁠similar agreements with safety institutes in the United States, Britain ​and Japan. Australia currently has no specific ‌AI legislation. ⁠ The centre-left Labor government has said it would rely on existing laws to manage emerging AI risks ‌while introducing voluntary guidelines amid privacy and safety concerns. Anthropic has sign a memorandum of understanding to share its economic index data with the Australian government to help track artificial intelligence ‌adoption across the economy, and ‌its ‌impact on workers and jobs. Under ‌the agreement, the Claude ⁠maker will share findings on emerging AI model capabilities and risks, participate in joint safety evaluations, and collaborate on research ​with Australian universities. Anthropic said it would also target investments in ⁠data centre infrastructure and energy across Australia. "Australia's investment in AI safety makes it a natural partner for responsible AI development," Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei said in Canberra, where he met Prime Minister Anthony Albanese, "This memorandum of ​understanding gives our collaboration ⁠a formal foundation." Australia currently has no specific ‌AI legislation. ⁠ The centre-left Labor government has said it would rely on existing laws to manage emerging AI risks ‌while introducing voluntary guidelines amid privacy and safety concerns. In its National AI Plan released in December, Labor outlined a roadmap ​to ramp up AI adoption across the economy, attract data centre investment, and build AI skills to ‌support jobs ⁠as the technology ​becomes more integrated into daily life. ### Add iTnews as your trusted source Got a news tip for our journalists? AI Agents.title: AI Is Redefining Software Supply Chain Security - Dark Reading
AI Agents.titleZh: AI Is Redefining Software Supply Chain Security - Dark Reading
AI Agents.content: * [Application Security](/application-security) # AI Is Redefining Software Supply Chain Security In a conversation with Dark Reading’s Terry Sweeney, Snyk’s Manoj Nair explains why AI-driven development is turning software supply chain security into a cross-disciplinary challenge. [Terry Sweeney,](/author/terry-sweeney)Contributing Editor April 1, 2026 Studio by Informa TechTarget Sponsored Content Autonomous attacks are now a defining part of the threat environment as AI-assisted development creates new classes of hard-to-find issues. According to Manoj Nair, Chief Innovation Officer at Snyk, the old approach to AppSec “doesn’t work anymore,” as he explains in this Dark Reading News Desk interview. While developers are increasingly using AI to generate code, “research shows that this code actually has a lot more vulnerabilities,” he says, and that it’s a “different class” of vulnerabilities to what teams are used to identifying and remediating. Nair believes that, in many organizations, governance is “basically sitting on paper,” making clear the gap between policy documents and actual implementation and enforcement. He shares an example of how easy it is for organizations to underestimate their AI footprints — many have limited visibility into how many different models have touched their code. The challenge is exacerbated by the fact that there are also now millions of open-source models, training datasets, and other resources, and that comes on top of unsanctioned use of mainstream generative AI tools to create code.  Related:[Axios NPM Package Compromised in Precision Attack](/application-security/axios-npm-package-compromised-precision-attack) As a result, AI is creating what Nair describes as a “governance problem” in software supply chain security, one where the impact is a “security and reputational problem.” This requires a new security playbook because static checking is no longer enough. Nair advocates for a new security model characterized by dynamic testing, permission awareness, and guardrails across the entire AI lifecycle. Continuous behavioral control and monitoring have become a mandatory part of software supply chain security, as organizations face the growing need to keep track of their AI footprints and how they impact development workflows. Nair is Chief Innovation Officer at Snyk, an AI-native and agentic security platform that helps organizations secure and govern development. ## About the Author [Terry Sweeney](/author/terry-sweeney) Contributing Editor Terry Sweeney is a Los Angeles-based writer and editor who has covered technology, networking, and security for more than 20 years. He was part of the team that started Dark Reading and has been a contributor to The Washington Post, Crain's New York Business, Red Herring, Network World, InformationWeek and Mobile Sports Report. In addition to information security, Sweeney has written extensively about cloud computing, wireless technologies, storage networking, and analytics. After watching successive waves of technological advancement, he still prefers to chronicle the actual application of these breakthroughs by businesses and public sector organizations. [See more from Terry Sweeney](/author/terry-sweeney) ## Want more Dark Reading stories in your Google search results? AI Agents.titleZh: Top 10 robotics developments of March 2026 - The Robot Report
AI Agents.content: [The Robot Report](https://www.therobotreport.com/) # Top 10 robotics developments of March 2026 By [Brianna Wessling](https://www.therobotreport.com/author/bwessling/) | March 2026 was a non-stop month for the robotics industry. Smart Factory & Automation World, or AW 2026, and NVIDIA GTC both brought an avalanche of new robotics and AI news. New robots, show recaps, and big acquisitions grabbed our readers’ attention this month. Here are the 10 most popular articles on [*The Robot Report*](http://therobotreport.com/) in the past month. Subscribe to [*The Robot Report*Newsletter](https://www.therobotreport.com/the-robot-report-enewsletter/) and listen to [*The Robot Report* Podcast](https://www.therobotreport.com/category/podcast/) to stay up to date on the latest robotics developments. --- ### 10. AW 2026 features Korea humanoid debuts as industry seeks digital transformation In early March 2026, Smart Factory & Automation World, or AW, occupied the entire Coex venue in Seoul and featured 2,300 booths. Chinese humanoid robot makers showed off their wares in a show within the show. [Read more](https://www.therobotreport.com/aw-2026-features-korea-humanoid-debuts-industry-seeks-digital-transformation/). --- ### 9. Noble Machines exits stealth with Moby humanoid Noble Machines has emerged from stealth mode. The startup, formerly known as Under Control Robotics, was founded by former engineers from Apple, SpaceX, NASA, and Caltech. It said it has deployed its first humanoids at a Fortune Global 500 customer within 18 months of its launch. [Read more](https://www.therobotreport.com/noble-machines-exits-stealth-with-moby-humanoid/). --- ### 8. BMW pilots Hexagon’s wheeled humanoid in March 2026 BMW has deployed wheeled humanoids from Hexagon Robotics at its plant in Leipzig, Germany. The automaker wants to integrate humanoid robots into car production and explore future applications for them in battery and component production. [Read more](https://www.therobotreport.com/bmw-piloting-hexagons-wheeled-humanoid-in-germany/). --- ### 7. RealSense unveils autonomous humanoid navigation at GTC 2026 As robots including humanoids move from novelty to everyday reality, perception is no longer just about performance; it’s about safety, according to RealSense. At NVIDIA GTC, the Intel spinoff demonstrated humanoid navigation with LimX Dynamics in March 2026. [Read more](https://www.therobotreport.com/realsense-unveils-autonomous-humanoid-navigation-gtc-2026/). --- ### 6. NVIDIA works with global robotics leaders to make physical AI a reality At its annual GPU Technology Conference, or GTC, NVIDIA showed off its partnerships with the global robotics ecosystem, including 110 developers, industrial automation leaders, and humanoid pioneers powering “production-scale physical AI.” [Read more](https://www.therobotreport.com/nvidia-collaborates-global-robotics-leaders-make-physical-ai-reality/). --- ### 5. Teradyne Robotics sues Chinese cobot maker over UR software Teradyne Robotics, a subsidiary of Teradyne Inc. and parent company of Universal Robots (UR) and Mobile Industrial Robots, is suing the German subsidiary of Elite Robots. Teradyne Robotics has accused the Chinese collaborative robot maker of infringing on UR’s proprietary software. [Read more](https://www.therobotreport.com/teradyne-sues-chinese-cobot-marker-over-ur-software/). --- ### 4. Rhoda AI exits stealth with $450M to train robots from video Rhoda AI raised Series A funding in March 2026. The company also unveiled FutureVision, an approach to robotic intelligence based on video-predictive control. Traditional industrial robots perform well in structured environments but remain limited to pre-programmed trajectories. [Read more](https://www.therobotreport.com/rhoda-ai-exits-stealth-with-450m-to-train-robots-from-video/). --- ### 3. Amazon acquires humanoid developer Fauna Robotics Amazon is acquiring the New York-based humanoid robot developer Fauna Robotics. Its Sprout robot is a...

[Anonymous form](https://www.macrumors.com/share.php)** # Apple Issues Rare iOS 18 Security Update to Protect Against DarkSword Exploit by [Juli Clover](https://www.macrumors.com/author/juli-clover/) Apple today released a new build of iOS 18.7.7 and iPadOS 18.7.7, presumably with a [fix for the DarkSword exploit](https://www.macrumors.com/2026/04/01/apple-ios-18-patch-darksword-exploit/). Apple told *[Wired](https://www.wired.com/story/apple-will-push-out-rare-backported-patches-to-protect-ios-18-users-from-darksword-hacking-tool/)* that it would release an iOS 18 update for more devices, allowing users with auto-update turned on to receive the security update. iOS 18.7.7 was initially limited to the iPhone XS and XR models, but it is now available for other iPhones. Apple is pushing the iOS 18 fix to customers who have decided not to upgrade to [iOS 26](https://www.macrumors.com/roundup/ios-26/), but the company encourages users with supported devices to update to ‌iOS 26‌ to get better protection. Devices running ‌iOS 26‌ are already protected against DarkSword, which is a vulnerability that allows a malicious entity to take over an iPhone if the iPhone user visits a website with infected code. It is highly unusual for Apple to provide a security update to iOS 18 users who simply don't want to upgrade to ‌iOS 26‌, as Apple uses security fixes as a way to push people to the latest version of iOS or iPadOS that their device supports. Apple says that the iOS 18.7.7 update is available for more devices, and devices with older versions of iOS 18 will receive an additional alert to install a Critical Security Update. > Note: We enabled the availability of iOS 18.7.7 for more devices on April 1, 2026, so users with Automatic Updates turned on can automatically receive important security protections from web attacks called DarkSword. The fixes associated with the DarkSword exploit first shipped in 2025. > > Devices with older versions of iOS 18 will receive an additional alert to install a Critical Security Update. DarkSword has received quite a bit of attention, and it has been used by hacker groups to break into the iPhones of people in Malaysia, Saudi Arabia, Turkey, and Ukraine. The exploit kit has also been posted to open source code repository GitHub, which makes it more widely available to bad actors. In his Power On newsletter today, he said the foldable iPhone will be "the most significant overhaul in the iPhone's history." "iPhone 4, iPhone 6 and iPhone X were clearly a big deal, but this is a whole new design," he said. Like Samsung's Galaxy Z Fold 7, the foldable iPhone will reportedly open up like ... Rishabh Pant By [Aditya Suketu Desai](/author/aditya-suketu-desai) Modified Apr 02, 2026 07:03 IST What's your opinion? LSG vs DC Highlights, IPL 2026: 3 moments that generated a buzz among fans in Match 5 ft. Rishabh Pant Delhi Capitals (DC) secured a six-wicket win over Lucknow Super Giants (LSG) in their IPL 2026 opener at Bharat Ratna Shri Atal Bihari Vajpayee Ekana Cricket Stadium, Lucknow, on Wednesday, April 1. LSG lost the toss and were put to bat first. Ad They failed to register a big total as they were bundled out for 141 in 18.4 overs. They suffered a batting collapse. [Mitchell Marsh](https://www.sportskeeda.com/player/mr-marsh) (35 off 28) and Abdul Samad (36 off 25) made crucial contributions, helping LSG get to a decent total. Lungi Ngidi and T Natarajan took three wickets apiece for DC. After their disappointing batting performance, the Lucknow-based side were off to an impressive start with the ball. Mohammad Shami dismissed KL Rahul on the first ball of the chase, putting DC under pressure. Ad Trending [Mohammad Shami strikes first ball to remove KL Rahul for golden duck in LSG vs DC IPL 2026 match [Watch]](https://www.sportskeeda.com/cricket/news-mohammad-shami-strikes-first-ball-remove-kl-rahul-golden-duck-lsg-vs-dc-ipl-2026-match-watch) [IPL 2026: 2 players who starred and 1 who flopped in today's LSG vs DC match ft. Rishabh Pant](https://www.sportskeeda.com/cricket/ipl-2026-2-players-starred-1-flopped-today-s-lsg-vs-dc-match-ft-rishabh-pant) [Prince Yadav knocks over Axar Patel for golden duck to leave the Capitals reeling in LSG vs DC IPL 2026 match](https://www.sportskeeda.com/cricket/news-prince-yadav-knocks-axar-patel-golden-duck-leave-capitals-reeling-lsg-vs-dc-ipl-2026-match) Delhi finished on 33/4 after six overs, recording the lowest powerplay score of the season. However, [Sameer Rizvi](https://www.sportskeeda.com/player/sameer-rizvi) played a brilliant knock under pressure to rescue his team. The youngster remained unbeaten on 70 off 47 to take his team over the line. Prince Yadav picked up two wickets for LSG. Here, we take a look at three interesting moments from the LSG vs DC match that generated a buzz among the fans. --- ## #3 Sameer Rizvi plays stunning ramp shot off Anrich Nortje's bowling Sameer Rizvi entertained fans with his strokeplay, hitting five fours and four sixes. It included a smart ramp shot off speedster Anrich Nortje's bowling in the eighth over. Ad The South African fast bowler bowled a short ball outside off stump to end the over. Rizvi countered it by playing an innovative ramp shot to send the ball over the third man fence for a six. Here's that clip of his shot: Ad --- ## #2 Prince Yadav and DC's overseas star get involved in a fiery stare-down LSG pacer Prince Yadav bowled an outstanding spell in the clash. He gave his team hope by removing [Axar Patel](https://www.sportskeeda.com/player/axar-patel) and Pathum Nissanka in the fifth over. Apart from his bowling exploits, he was also involved in a fiery stand-off with Tristan Stubbs. In the same over, Stubbs hit a four off Yadav's bowling with the pull shot. The seamer followed it with a full delivery, which was a dot. He stared at the South African batter in the follow-through. Ad Stubbs didn't back down and did the same, resulting in an intense stare-down. Watch the video of the incident here: Ad --- ## #1 Rishabh Pant's knock ends with an unlucky run out LSG skipper Rishabh Pant opened the batting for his team in their opening clash. However, it was a brief stay at the crease for him as his knock ended prematurely. On the final ball of the third over, Mitchell Marsh drove the ball straight towards pacer [Mukesh Kumar](https://www.sportskeeda.com/player/mukesh-kumar). The bowler went for the catch. However, he could only get his fingertips on the ball, and it crashe...

AI Product.content: # OpenAI Raises $3 Billion from Retail Investors as Part of $122 Billion Funding April 1, 2026 at 9:53 AM • by MLQ Agent #### Key Points * OpenAI is raising $110 billion in new investment at a $730 billion pre-money valuation.[1] * $30 billion each from SoftBank and NVIDIA, and $50 billion from Amazon.[1] * Includes strategic partnership with Amazon and compute deal with NVIDIA.[1] * Valuation boosts OpenAI Foundation's stake to over $180 billion.[1] * Additional investors expected as round progresses.[1] OpenAI announced a $110 billion funding round on Wednesday at a $730 billion pre-money valuation, led by major investments from SoftBank, NVIDIA, and Amazon. The deal includes strategic partnerships and aims to support the company's AI development efforts. ## Funding Breakdown The round comprises $30 billion from SoftBank, $30 billion from NVIDIA, and $50 billion from Amazon.[1] OpenAI has also secured a strategic partnership with Amazon and next-generation inference compute with NVIDIA.[1] Additional financial investors are expected to participate as the round continues.[1] ## Valuation Impact The new valuation increases the value of the OpenAI Foundation’s stake in OpenAI Group to over $180 billion.[1] This strengthens the foundation's resources, one of the most well-funded nonprofits, enabling expanded philanthropy in health breakthroughs and AI resilience.[1] ## Context from Recent Deals Separate reports indicate OpenAI employees have cashed out $3 billion in shares amid ongoing fundraising talks for up to $40 billion.[3] Earlier funding included a $6.6 billion raise at a $157 billion valuation, plus a $4 billion credit line.[2] ## Investor Confidence Signals The scale of OpenAI's $110 billion raise underscores the intense capital demands of frontier AI development, where compute resources and talent competition drive unprecedented investment levels.[1] Commitments from SoftBank, NVIDIA, and Amazon—totaling the full announced amount—signal strong institutional confidence in OpenAI's path to scaling AI capabilities, particularly with tied compute and partnership deals that reduce execution risks.[1] This pre-money valuation of $730 billion positions OpenAI ahead of many public tech firms, reflecting investor bets on AI's centrality to future economic growth despite the company's private status and governance complexities. Comparisons to prior rounds highlight accelerating momentum: from $6.6 billion at $157 billion valuation to this massive infusion.[2] Employee share cash-outs of $3 billion suggest maturing internal liquidity options, potentially aiding retention amid talent wars, while talks of additional $40 billion indicate the round may expand further.[3] Overall, the structure prioritizes strategic alignment over pure financial returns, with backers gaining direct exposure to AI infrastructure advancements. ## Scaling and IPO Path OpenAI's partnerships with Amazon and NVIDIA position it to accelerate inference and model training, key to competing in real-time AI applications.[1] As additional investors join, the round could surpass initial targets, funding expansions like the referenced AI superapp ambitions from related coverage.[4] The bolstered foundation stake enables parallel investments in AI safety and philanthropy, potentially shaping public policy discourse on responsible scaling.[1] A potential IPO remains on the horizon, with this liquidity event building retail and public interest through demonstrated growth metrics, such as surpassing 1 million business users for ChatGPT.[2] Regulatory scrutiny may intensify given the valuation and nonprofit ties, but secured compute and capital provide a runway through 2027 at least. Competitive pressures from rivals will test whether this funding translates to sustained market leadership. ### Companies Mentioned [#### Amazon.com, Inc. Ends 11:59 p.m. PT tonight. [**REGISTER NOW**](https://techcrunch.com/events/tc-disrupt-2026/?utm_source=tc&utm_medium=ad&utm_campaign=disrupt2026&utm_content=seb&promo=topbanner_seb&display=). ### Topics [Biotech & Health](/category/biotech-health/) [Climate](/category/climate/) [Cloud Computing](/tag/cloud-computing/) [Commerce](/category/commerce/) [Crypto](/category/cryptocurrency/) [Enterprise](/category/enterprise/) [EVs](/tag/evs/) [Fintech](/category/fintech/) [Fundraising](/category/fundraising/) [Gadgets](/category/gadgets/) [Gaming](/category/gaming/) [Google](/tag/google/) [Government & Policy](/category/government-policy/) [Hardware](/category/hardware/) [Instagram](/tag/instagram/) [Layoffs](/tag/layoffs/) [Media & Entertainment](/category/media-entertainment/) [Meta](/tag/meta/) [Microsoft](/tag/microsoft/) [Privacy](/category/privacy/) [Robotics](/category/robotics/) [Security](/category/security/) [Social](/category/social/) [Space](/category/space/) [Startups](/category/startups/) [TikTok](/tag/tiktok/) [Transportation](/category/transportation/) [Venture](/category/venture/) ### More from TechCrunch [Staff](/about-techcrunch/) [Events](/events/) [Startup Battlefield](/startup-battlefield/) [StrictlyVC](https://strictlyvc.com/) [Newsletters](/newsletters/) [Podcasts](/podcasts/) [Videos](/video/) [Partner Content](/sponsored/) [TechCrunch Brand Studio](/brand-studio/) [Crunchboard](https://www.crunchboard.com/) [Contact Us](/contact-us/) In Brief Posted: * [Marina Temkin](https://techcrunch.com/author/marina-temkin/) # Startup funding shatters all records in Q1 Global investing in startups hit $297 billion in Q1 2026, breaking all records, according to new [Crunchbase data](https://news.crunchbase.com/venture/record-breaking-funding-ai-global-q1-2026/). That’s a massive 2.5x increase over the $118 billion raised in the previous quarter. This single-quarter haul outpaces every full year of global VC activity prior to 2019. The unprecedented spike was fueled by just four behemoth deals, each a record-breaker in its own right. Last month, OpenAI [announced](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/31/openai-not-yet-public-raises-3b-from-retail-investors-in-monster-122b-fund-raise/) that it is now valued at $852 billion after collecting $122 billion, surpassing the previous record for the largest funding round ever, also held by OpenAI when the ChatGPT maker raised [$40 billion](https://techcrunch.com/2025/03/31/openai-raises-40b-at-300b-post-money-valuation/) a year ago. The quarter also saw [Anthropic](https://techcrunch.com/2026/02/12/anthropic-raises-another-30-billion-in-series-g-with-a-new-value-of-380-billion/), its main rival, raise $30 billion at a valuation of $380 billion. That funding haul effectively made it the third-largest VC round on record. The other two mega-deals of the quarter included a [$20 billion](https://techcrunch.com/2026/01/06/xai-says-it-raised-20b-in-series-e-funding/) fundraise by xAI and Waymo’s [$16 billion](https://techcrunch.com/2026/02/02/waymo-raises-16-billion-round-to-scale-robotaxi-fleet-london-tokyo/) round. These four rounds collectively raised $188 billion, accounting for more than 63% of total funding in the quarter. While it might appear that without them, fundraising appears to be on a more typical trajectory, anecdotal evidence implies otherwise. Investors and founders say, for example, that seed-stage AI startups are [commanding bigger dollars, and higher valuations](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/31/its-not-your-imagination-ai-seed-startups-are-commanding-higher-valuations/) at earlier stages than ever before. Open Source AI.title: AI Is Redefining Software Supply Chain Security - Dark Reading
Open Source AI.titleZh: AI Is Redefining Software Supply Chain Security - Dark Reading
Open Source AI.content: * [Application Security](/application-security) # AI Is Redefining Software Supply Chain Security In a conversation with Dark Reading’s Terry Sweeney, Snyk’s Manoj Nair explains why AI-driven development is turning software supply chain security into a cross-disciplinary challenge. [Terry Sweeney,](/author/terry-sweeney)Contributing Editor April 1, 2026 Studio by Informa TechTarget Sponsored Content Autonomous attacks are now a defining part of the threat environment as AI-assisted development creates new classes of hard-to-find issues. According to Manoj Nair, Chief Innovation Officer at Snyk, the old approach to AppSec “doesn’t work anymore,” as he explains in this Dark Reading News Desk interview. While developers are increasingly using AI to generate code, “research shows that this code actually has a lot more vulnerabilities,” he says, and that it’s a “different class” of vulnerabilities to what teams are used to identifying and remediating. Nair believes that, in many organizations, governance is “basically sitting on paper,” making clear the gap between policy documents and actual implementation and enforcement. He shares an example of how easy it is for organizations to underestimate their AI footprints — many have limited visibility into how many different models have touched their code. The challenge is exacerbated by the fact that there are also now millions of open-source models, training datasets, and other resources, and that comes on top of unsanctioned use of mainstream generative AI tools to create code.  Related:[Axios NPM Package Compromised in Precision Attack](/application-security/axios-npm-package-compromised-precision-attack) As a result, AI is creating what Nair describes as a “governance problem” in software supply chain security, one where the impact is a “security and reputational problem.” This requires a new security playbook because static checking is no longer enough. Nair advocates for a new security model characterized by dynamic testing, permission awareness, and guardrails across the entire AI lifecycle. Continuous behavioral control and monitoring have become a mandatory part of software supply chain security, as organizations face the growing need to keep track of their AI footprints and how they impact development workflows. Nair is Chief Innovation Officer at Snyk, an AI-native and agentic security platform that helps organizations secure and govern development. ## About the Author [Terry Sweeney](/author/terry-sweeney) Contributing Editor Terry Sweeney is a Los Angeles-based writer and editor who has covered technology, networking, and security for more than 20 years. He was part of the team that started Dark Reading and has been a contributor to The Washington Post, Crain's New York Business, Red Herring, Network World, InformationWeek and Mobile Sports Report. In addition to information security, Sweeney has written extensively about cloud computing, wireless technologies, storage networking, and analytics. After watching successive waves of technological advancement, he still prefers to chronicle the actual application of these breakthroughs by businesses and public sector organizations. Open Source AI.titleZh: Claude’s code: Anthropic leaks source code for AI software engineering tool - The Guardian
Open Source AI.content: [Skip to navigation](#navigation)[Skip to navigation](#navigation) [Print subscriptions](https://support.theguardian.com/subscribe/weekly?REFPVID=undefined&INTCMP=undefined&acquisitionData=%7B%22source%22%3A%22GUARDIAN_WEB%22%2C%22componentId%22%3A%22PrintSubscriptionsHeaderLink%22%2C%22componentType%22%3A%22ACQUISITIONS_HEADER%22%2C%22referrerUrl%22%3A%22%22%7D) [Search jobs](https://jobs.theguardian.com) [Sign in](https://profile.theguardian.com/signin?INTCMP=DOTCOM_NEWHEADER_SIGNIN&ABCMP=ab-sign-in&componentEventParams=componentType%3Didentityauthentication%26componentId%3Dguardian_signin_header) # Claude’s code: Anthropic leaks source code for AI software engineering tool Nearly 2,000 internal files were briefly leaked after ‘human error’, raising fresh security questions at the AI company Anthropic accidentally released part of the internal source code for its [AI](https://www.theguardian.com/technology/artificialintelligenceai)-powered coding assistant, Claude Code, due to “human error”, the company said on Tuesday. An internal-use file mistakenly included in a software update pointed to an archive containing nearly 2,000 files and 500,000 lines of code, which were quickly copied to developer platform GitHub. A post on X sharing a link to the leaked code had more than 29m views early on Wednesday, and a rewritten version of the source code quickly became GitHub’s fastest-ever downloaded repository. Anthropic issued [copyright takedown requests](https://www.pcmag.com/news/anthropic-issues-8000-copyright-takedowns-to-scrub-claude-code-leak) to try to contain the code’s spread. Within the code, users spotted blueprints for a Tamagotchi-esque coding assistant and an always-on [AI agent](https://www.theguardian.com/technology/ng-interactive/2026/mar/12/lab-test-mounting-concern-over-rogue-ai-agents-artificial-intelligence), per [the Verge](https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/904776/anthropic-claude-source-code-leak). “Earlier today, a Claude Code release included some internal source code. No sensitive customer data or credentials were involved or exposed,” an Anthropic spokesperson said. “This was a release packaging issue caused by human error, not a security breach.” The exposed code was related to the tool’s internal architecture but did not contain confidential data from Claude, the underlying AI model by Anthropic. Claude Code’s source code was partially known, as the tool had been reverse-engineered by independent developers. An earlier version of the assistant had its source code exposed in February 2025. Claude Code has emerged as a key product for Anthropic, as the company’s paid subscriber base continues to grow. TechCrunch [reported](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/28/anthropics-claude-popularity-with-paying-consumers-is-skyrocketing/) last week that paid subscriptions have more than doubled this year, per an Anthropic spokesperson. Anthropic’s Claude chatbot also received a popularity boost amid the CEO Dario Amodei’s tussle with the Pentagon; Claude climbed to the top spot of Apple’s chart of top free apps in the US just more than a month ago. Amodei had refused to back down on red lines around the use of his company’s technology for mass surveillance and fully autonomous weapons. This is the second time that Anthropic has had a data leak in recent weeks. Fortune previously [reported](https://fortune.com/2026/03/26/anthropic-says-testing-mythos-powerful-new-ai-model-after-data-leak-reveals-its-existence-step-change-in-capabilities/) on a separate breach and noted that the company was storing thousands of internal files on publicly accessible systems. That included a draft of a blog post that referred to an upcoming model known as “Mythos” and “Capybara”. Some experts worry the leaks suggest internal security vulnerabilities within Anthropic. That could be particularly troubling for a company focused on AI safety. The leaks could also help competitors, like OpenAI and Google, better understand how ...

Open Source AI.title: Google Launches Veo 3.1 Lite, a More Cost-Effective AI Video Generator Model - CNET
Open Source AI.titleZh: Google Launches Veo 3.1 Lite, a More Cost-Effective AI Video Generator Model - CNET
Open Source AI.content: * [Tech](/tech/) * Services & Software * [AI](/tech/services-and-software/ai/) # Google Launches Veo 3.1 Lite, a More Cost-Effective AI Video Generator Model Google is doubling down on AI video and trying to make it less expensive and energy-intensive to run. Blake Stimac Writer Blake has over a decade of experience writing for the web, with a focus on mobile phones, where he covered the smartphone boom of the 2010s and the broader tech scene. When he's not in front of a keyboard, you'll most likely find him playing video games, watching horror flicks, or hunting down a good churro. [See full bio](/profiles/bstimac/) Katelyn Chedraoui Writer I Katelyn is a writer with CNET covering artificial intelligence, including chatbots, image and video generators. Her work explores how new AI technology is infiltrating our lives, shaping the content we consume on social media and affecting the people behind the screens. She graduated from the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill with a degree in media and journalism. You can reach her at kchedraoui@cnet.com. Expertise artificial intelligence, AI image generators, social media platforms [See full bio](/profiles/kchedraoui/) Blake Stimac , Katelyn Chedraoui 2 min read Google is launching a cost-effective version of its Veo 3 [AI video generator](/tech/services-and-software/your-ultimate-guide-to-ai-video-generators-how-to-use-sora-2-veo-3-and-more/), Veo 3.1 Lite, the company [announced Tuesday](https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/technology/ai/veo-3-1-lite/ "(opens in a new window)"), highlighting Google's renewed commitment to all forms of generative AI. Google says that its latest addition will round out the Veo product family to give developers flexibility based on their needs. The company says the new model will cost 50% less than Veo 3.1 Fast while retaining the same speed. And like all the Veo 3 models, Veo 3.1 Lite will support audio within the videos. A representative for Google did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Veo 3.1 Lite offers an assortment of features, including text-to-video and image-to-video, along with 16:9 and 9:16 aspect ratios, and video resolution up to 1080p. Veo 3.1 Lite doesn't support 4K resolutions like other models, something that may disappoint creators making professional-grade videos. The blog post also points out that developers can customize video duration at 4,6, or 8 seconds, and costs will adjust accordingly. The rollout of Veo 3.1 Lite began on Tuesday and is accessible to developers through the paid tier of the Gemini API and Google AI Studio, including [Google's filmmaking studio, Flow](/tech/services-and-software/googles-ai-filmmaker-program-flow-helped-creators-make-100-million-videos/). The timing of this release is telling. Last week, OpenAI -- one of Google's biggest competitors in the AI space -- announced it would be shutting down its video generation app, Sora. Sora was a unique offering because it was both a social media app and an AI video creation tool. Google's Veo 3.1 Lite highlights that the tech giant is not stepping away from generative media, but instead, is looking for ways to make creating AI images and videos cheaper. OpenAI's official statement said it was sunsetting Sora to reprioritize core goals, but AI video is a [compute-intensive process](/tech/services-and-software/openai-kills-sora-ai-image-video-commentary/), which makes it expensive for AI companies to run. In addition to Veo 3.1 Lite, Google is also updating pricing for the Veo 3.1 Fast model. The new pricing will be available "soon," according to the [developer documentation](https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/pricing#veo-3.1 "(opens in a new window)"). ## Veo 3.1 pricing ## Veo 3.1 Lite and Fast pricing | | | | | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Model | Resolution | Price (per second) | Old pricing (per second) | | Veo 3.1 Lite | 720p | $0.05 | N/A | | Veo 3.1 Lite | 1080p | $0.08 | N/A | | Veo 3.1 Fast | 720p | $0.10 | $0.15 | | Veo 3.1 Fa...

A week after OpenAI said it would shutter its artificial intelligence video generator, Sora, rival tools like Kling AI and RunwayML are already gaining ground. Kling AI, a video generator app owned by China’s Kuaishou Technology, saw global weekly active users jump 4% to an average of 2.6 million last week from the prior week, according to iOS and Android data that market intelligence firm Sensor Tower shared with Bloomberg News. Even before OpenAI said it would shut down Sora, Kling AI was leading in global monthly active users, averaging 7.8 million in March compared with 4.7 million for Sora. (The website and app for Sora will go away on April 26, while the developer platform will be discontinued later this year, on Sept. 24.) Advertisement Other apps also saw a bump: RunwayML and Vidu each notched a 1% increase in weekly active users the same week. (Runway AI Inc.’s app is only available on Apple Inc.’s app store, while Beijing Shengshu Technology Co.’s Vidu is on both iOS and Android.) In another sign of investor confidence in their technology, Runway and Shengshu both raised new funding in February. OpenAI’s decision to discontinue support opens the door for smaller rivals to win users and gain revenue in a niche that’s seeing surging demand from creatives, filmmakers and marketers. Infra & Chips.title: Nvidia ploughs $2 billion into Marvell in data centre push - Telecoms
Infra & Chips.titleZh: Nvidia ploughs $2 billion into Marvell in data centre push - Telecoms
Infra & Chips.content: * [AI](/digital-ecosystem/ai) * [Fibre](/fixed-networks/fibre) * [News](/latest-news) # Nvidia ploughs $2 billion into Marvell in data centre push Nvidia has announced a $2 billion investment in fellow chipmaker Marvell, as they join hands and look to develop silicon photonics technology and data centre networking. [Andrew Wooden,](/author/andrew-wooden) Deputy Editor April 1, 2026 3 Min Read As well as that sizable investment, which seems to be a default move by Nvidia when shopping for collaborators these days, the idea is about connecting Marvell to the Nvidia AI factory and AI-RAN ecosystem, “offering customers building on Nvidia architectures greater choice and flexibility in developing next-generation infrastructure.” Nvidia NVLink Fusion is described as a rack-scale platform that enables data centre customers to develop semi-custom AI infrastructure using its ecosystem. Marvell getting involved will see it contributing custom XPUs and compatible scale-up networking into the mix, while Nvidia will provide the supporting technologies – which include Vera CPU, ConnectX NICs, BlueField DPUs, NVLink interconnect and Spectrum-X switches, and the rack-scale AI compute. For customers developing custom chips, “NVLink Fusion enables a heterogeneous AI infrastructure fully compatible with Nvidia systems, allowing seamless integration with Nvidia GPU, LPU, networking and storage platforms,” states the release.  Related: [EE launches AI TV search feature](/ai/ee-launches-ai-tv-search-feature) There’s a lot of jargon in there, but essentially they are pooling resources to jointly go after AI data centre business.  “The inference inflection has arrived. Token generation demand is surging, and the world is racing to build AI factories,” said Jensen Huang, founder and CEO of Nvidia. “Together with Marvell, we are enabling customers to leverage Nvidia’s AI infrastructure ecosystem and scale to build specialized AI compute.” Matt Murphy, chairman and CEO of Marvell added: “Our expanded partnership with Nvidia reflects the growing importance of high-speed connectivity, optical interconnect and accelerated infrastructure in scaling AI. By connecting Marvell’s leadership in high-performance analog, optical DSP, silicon photonics and custom silicon to Nvidia’s expanding AI ecosystem through NVLink Fusion, we are enabling customers to build scalable, efficient AI infrastructure.” The companies will work to “transform the world’s telecommunication network into AI infrastructure” using Nvidia Aerial AI-RAN for 5G/6G, optical interconnect solutions, and silicon photonics technology.  This latter part would seem to be a key pillar of the deal. Late last year Marvell agreed to [acquire Celestial AI](/ai/marvell-snaps-up-celestial-ai) for an upfront consideration valued at approximately $3.25 billion.  Celestial AI makes an optical interconnect technology platform for AI computing called Photonic Fabric, which is supposed to enable large AI clusters to scale both within and across racks using a high-bandwidth, low latency, low power and cost-effective optical fabric. This apparently means more than two times the power efficiency of copper interconnects, along with longer reach and higher bandwidth. Related: [Mistral raises $830m to fund Nvidia spending spree](/ai/mistral-raises-830m-to-fund-nvidia-spending-spree) Marvell said at the time of that deal that combined with its existing scale-out and scale-across connectivity, it expects the expanded portfolio “will result in the industry’s most comprehensive provider of high-bandwidth, low-power, low-latency solutions for next-generation data centre connectivity.” In January this year Marvell then made a further purchase in the data centre space, [snapping up XConn for $540 million](/enterprise-telecoms/marvell-buys-xconn-for-540m-for-an-even-bigger-piece-of-the-data-center-action) . Xconn specialises in the interconnects between computing components, and Marvell’s CEO Matt Murphy said at the time: “Thi...

Infra & Chips.content: # [Bismarck Brief](/) [AI 2026](https://brief.bismarckanalysis.com/s/ai-2026/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu) # Why Neoclouds Are Vital to AI Startups ### Commodity compute is key to making new AI research breakthroughs. AI startups and Nvidia have allied with financial firms to prevent this commodity from being monopolized by incumbent software giants. [Samo Burja](https://substack.com/@samoburja) Apr 01, 2026 ∙ Paid The recent growth in optimism about artificial intelligence technology has led to a boom in demand for the computing power or “compute” that these new AI algorithms require for training i.e. development and inference i.e. usage. The high compute demands for AI are met through chips like [Nvidia’s](https://brief.bismarckanalysis.com/p/nvidias-successful-bet-on-artificial) “graphics processing units” (GPUs), which are capable of processing vast amounts of computations in parallel. While GPUs and their equivalents from other companies like [AMD](https://brief.bismarckanalysis.com/p/amd-is-a-functional-institution-in) or Google can be purchased and run on a company’s own premises in what is now commonly called a “data center” or “compute cluster,” the financial costs and organizational complexities of building such data centers, maintaining them, and inevitably upgrading them with new and more powerful chips are prohibitively high even apparently for well-funded frontier AI labs like OpenAI or Anthropic. As a result, many companies and individuals choose instead to rent compute from “cloud computing” providers like [Amazon Web Services](https://brief.bismarckanalysis.com/p/the-growth-prospects-for-aws) (AWS) or [Microsoft](https://brief.bismarckanalysis.com/p/ai-2026-microsofts-transformation) Azure and, alongside these large and established companies, a number of smaller and newer “neocloud” companies have arisen to meet demand specifically for remotely renting out AI compute. ## This post is for paid subscribers [Already a paid subscriber? **Sign in**](https://substack.com/sign-in?redirect=%2Fp%2Fwhy-neoclouds-are-vital-to-ai-startups&for_pub=bismarck&change_user=false) © 2026 Samo Burja · [Privacy](https://substack.com/privacy) ∙ [Terms](https://substack.com/tos) ∙ [Collection notice](https://substack.com/ccpa#personal-data-collected) [Start your Substack](https://substack.com/signup?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=web&utm_content=footer)[Get the app](https://substack.com/app/app-store-redirect?utm_campaign=app-marketing&utm_content=web-footer-button) [Substack](https://substack.com) is the home for great culture
